summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – Where the father seeks to vary interim parenting orders in the shadow of a final trial and other relief absent particularity and evidentiary foundation – Application in a Proceeding refused – No matters of principle.

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

CAMPTON J:

Mr Caivano (“the father”) and Ms Thompson (“the mother”) are engaged in proceedings as to the parenting of their child, X (“the child”) born in 2020.
The father, by way of his Amended Initiating Application filed on 17 January 2025, seeks orders on a final basis that the parties share responsibility for long-term decision-making for the child’s education, religious upbringing, and healthcare; that the child live with the mother; that he spend time with the child on a monthly basis, from the conclusion of daycare on Friday until the Monday of the following week, and otherwise have specified days during the year; and for he to communicate with the child by way of telephone and video call.
The mother, by way of her Response to an Initiating Application filed on 5 February 2025, seeks orders that she be solely responsible for the long-term decision-making for the child, that the child live with her, and that there be no time spent or communication between the father and the child.
The parties entered consent orders on 29 April 2025, relevant for the current interlocutory application to vary, as follows:
Responsibility for Decision Making
That the mother have sole decision-making responsibility for all major long term issues relating to the child, [X] born [...] 2020.

Live with and spend time

2. [X] shall live with the mother.

3. [X] shall spend time with the father as follows: -
Supervised by an independent supervision service in the [Suburb C] area [in] [Region D] (Contact Service), for a period of two (2) hours once per month on the last weekend of every month (or subject to the availability of the Contact Centre) with the father to absorb the costs.
b. For the purpose of this Order: -
Within fourteen (14) days the mother shall Contact a Contact Service [in] [Region D].
ii. The parties will thereafter: -
Arrange an appointment for assessment of suitability for supervised contact.
Attend any such assessment required by the Contact Service.
Comply with the requirements of the Contact Service.
Comply with all reasonable rules of the Contact Service.
Comply with all reasonable requests or directions of the staff of the Contact Service
Other
That within seven (7) days of these Orders the father will attend on a GP for a mental health plan and obtain a referral to see a psychologist or psychiatrist and continue to attend upon them as recommended.
The mother will within fourteen (14) days of these Orders make proper arrangements for [X] to engage with a child psychologist and thereafter facilitate [sic] [X’s] attendance on same.
Within seven (7) days of these Orders the father shall enrol in the following courses and thereafter complete them and provide evidence of completion to the other party and/or their solicitor and the ICL (if required): -
https://tuningintokids.org.au/parents/
https:// /taking-responsibility-mens-behaviour-change/
https:// group-workshops/managing-anger/

Restraints
Pursuant to section 68B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), the father be restrained by injunction from: -
a. Approaching within 100m of: -
i. The mother’s residence.

ii. The mother’s place of employment.

iii. The residence of the maternal grandparents.

iv. [X’s] daycare.

v. [X’s] school.
b. Contacting the mother or [X].
Pursuant to section 68B of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), the mother be restrained by injunction without admissions from: -

a. Approaching within 100m of: -
i. The father’s residence.

ii. The father’s place of work.
IT IS FURTHER ORDERED THAT:

2. All outstanding interim applications are marked as finalised.

(Emphasis in original)
Three occasions of supervised time between the father and the child have occurred pursuant to the orders made on 29 April 2025. The mother thereafter ceased to facilitate time pursuant to the orders.
The father filed an Application in a Proceeding on 27 November 2025, as amended by way of an Application in a Proceeding filed on 22 December 2025 seeking:
That an urgent psychological assessment of the child, [X], be conducted by an independent psychologist to determine the cause of any distress and to recommend appropriate contact arrangements within 28 days of the date of these Orders, with the report to be made available to both parties.
That joint Father-Child counselling sessions be arranged on a monthly basis to support and facilitate contact arrangements.
That supervised contact between the Applicant Father and the child resume immediately in Sydney or another mutually agreed location, with the costs of supervision and/or travel to be shared equitably between the parties.
That the Respondent Mother provide any supporting evidence regarding the child’s alleged distress within 14 days of the date of these orders, failing which the Court may draw an adverse inference as to the veracity of her claims.
Declare that the Mother’s refusal to facilitate the Child’s attendance at the joint Father-Child session during the preparation of the s 62G Report on 25 November 2025 pursuant to order made on 2 September 2025 was without reasonable excuse and has compromised the utility of that report.
A private report writer to be agreed upon by the Parties, or failing agreement to be selected by the Court from a panel of three names provided by the Applicant Father, be appointed as a single expert to prepare a family report in lieu of (or otherwise in addition to) the compromised s 62G Report ordered on 2 September 2025.
Both parties and the Child attend upon the single expert at all times and places appointed, and the Mother is hereby restrained from withholding the Child from any such appointment, including the joint Father-Child session.
The single expert be requested inter alia to conduct and report upon observed joint interaction between the Father and the Child.
The costs of the single expert report be paid to the Respondent Mother on the basis that the respondent Mother restrained the child from attending the joint Father-Child session during the preparation of the s 62G report on 25 November 2025 without reasonable excuse.
The Respondent Mother pay the Applicant Father’s costs of and incidental to this application on the basis that the Mother has unilaterally terminated supervised contact without reasonable excuse and intereferred [sic] with the preparation of the s 62G report without reasonable excuse.
Such further or other orders as the Court deems fit and in the best interests of the Child.
(Underline emphasis removed)
The mother filed a Response to an Application in a Proceeding on 4 December 2025, seeking that the order for supervised time made on 29 April 2025 be discharged, and that the father's Application in a Proceeding be otherwise dismissed.
These reasons determine the father’s Application in a Proceeding as amended and the mother’s Response to an Application in a Proceeding.

FURTHER BACKGROUND

On 17 February 2026, as amended on 19 February 2026, a senior judicial registrar made the following orders:
PENDING FURTHER ORDER, THE COURT ORDERS THAT:
The Application in a Proceeding filed 27 November 2025 is dismissed together with Orders 1-4 5-11 inclusive in the Amended Application in a Proceeding filed 22 December 2025.

Updating Section 67ZBE Material – NSW Police Force
Further to Orders made on 18 December 2024 and pursuant to section 67ZBE of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth), the NSW Police Force is to provide to the Court by no later than 4.00pm on 17 March 2026 any documents and any information in its possession or control relating to the matters in section 67ZBE(2) in relation to the following people for the period 19 December 2024 to date:
a. [X] born [...] 2020 (child);
Mr [Caivano] born [...] 1981 (father of the child); and

c. Ms [Thompson] born [...] 1981(mother of the child).
The parties be restrained from issuing any subsequent subpoena to NSW Police seeking the production of any information or documents identified pursuant to these orders, except with the leave of the Court.
Transfer to Division 1
Pursuant to section 149 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia Act 2021 (Cth), this proceeding is transferred to the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Division 1).

Listings
The directions hearing listed before Judicial Registrar Lovell-Jones on 23 February 2026 is hereby vacated.
The Compliance and Readiness Hearing before Judge Murdoch on 6 May 2026 is hereby vacated.
The matter remains listed for Subpoena Mention before Judicial Registrar Lovell-Jones at 12:00pm on 23 March 2026 (via Webex).
The matter may be listed in Division 1 for case management on 48 hours’ notice to the parties.

IT IS NOTED THAT:
There is a pending interim application filed 14 November 2025 and amended on 22 December 2025 by the Applicant Father.
The Court notes that the Father has filed 2 subsequent Application in a Proceedings (not including his original interim application contained in his application for final orders). Therefore, attention is drawn to 6.17(b) of the Central Practice Direction - Family Law Case Management.
The Court explained to the Father the reason for the dismissal of the Application in a Proceeding filed 27 November 2025 and dismissal of proposed orders 5-10 5-11 inclusive of the Amended Application of 22 December 2025, as they are not orders that the Court could make at an interim hearing or appropriate at the time as the Family Report had not yet been released.
The Father may press an interim hearing in regard to his Amended Application dated 22 December 2025 orders 1-4 inclusive. The mother relies on her Response to the Application in a Proceeding filed 4 December 2025.
A Child Impact Report was released (restricted) on 15 April 2025. Orders have been made today for the Father to inspect a copy of the Child Impact Report in the Registry as he is a self-represented litigant.
The Family Report dated 12 December 2025 has been released today by the Court (restricted). The Release of the Family Report orders are contained in a separate set of Orders dated today 17 February 2026. The Father is permitted to inspect the Family Report in the Registry should he wish. In the event the Father becomes legally represented, then his legal representative is permitted to obtain copies of both reports.
The Father has read the Family Report today with the assistance of a FASS worker.
The Father is currently not spending supervised time with the child as per the orders dated 29 April 2025 which provided for the Father to spend time with the child once per month. The parties have accessed two supervision services to date.
The Mother seeks orders on an interim and final basis that the child [X], born [...] 2020 (currently aged 5 years), spend no time with the Father.
The child was unable to be observed during the Family Report process with the Father.
There is an expired ADVO for the protection of the Mother. The Court will need to consider the various provisions of Section 102NA(1) of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) prior to trial.
The Father had the assistance of a Family Advocacy and Support Services worker (FASS) at the hearing.

Updating Section 67ZBE Material – NSW Police Force
The documents to be provided by the NSW Police Force pursuant to Order 2 above may relate to documents or information relating to the following matters set out in section 67ZBE(2):
abuse, neglect or family violence to which a child to whom the proceedings relate has been, or is suspected to have been, subjected or exposed;
family violence to which a party to the proceedings has been exposed, or in which a party to the proceedings has engaged, to the extent it may affect a child to whom the proceedings relate;
any risk or potential risk of a child to whom the proceedings relate being subjected or exposed to abuse, neglect or family violence;
any risk or potential risk of a party to the proceedings being subjected to, or engaging in, family violence, to the extent any such family violence may affect a child to whom the proceedings relate.
Pursuant to s 67ZBK, the parties are restrained from issuing any subsequent subpoena to the NSW Police Force requiring the NSW Police Force to provide documents or information in relation to these proceedings, except with the leave of the Court.
Material may be redacted pursuant to s 67ZBF.
Pursuant to s 67ZBH(1) the court must admit into evidence any particulars, documents or information provided under an order made under section 67ZBE, or under subsection 67ZBE(5), on which the court intends to rely.

Transfer to Division 1
The matter has been transferred to Division 1 for the following reasons:
The serious allegations of family violence.
Where the Family Report Writer Ms [B] makes recommendations for the child to have no time and no communication with the Father.
The Applicant Father is a self-represented litigant.
The duration of trial.
The parties will be contacted directly by the Court confirming the next listing date.

(As per the original)
As indicated, the parties have the benefit of a recent comprehensive Family Report.
The father asserts that the mother is in contravention of the interlocutory parenting orders made on 29 April 2025. He has not filed a contravention application.
His relief by way of his Amended Application in a Proceeding that remains outstanding, as at today, does not engage with a contended contravention of a parenting order by way of the relevant sections of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth).
He asserts that the mother’s refusal to facilitate the child’s attendance at an observation for the purposes of the preparation of the Family Report has compromised the contents, evidence and opinions contained within the report.

CONSIDERATION

The purposes of the father’s interlocutory relief as sought

The father was unable to particularise during the hearing today the identity of the individual with the requisite expertise to undertake “an urgent psychological assessment of” the child so as to “determine the cause of any distress” contended to be exhibited by the child. Unsurprisingly, he has not adduced evidence as to the information to be provided to the expert undertaking the assessment, or when that assessment would be undertaken, or otherwise as to how his order sought complies with ch 7 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) for the purposes of adducing evidence from the individual undertaking the assessment at trial.
The determination as to the fact and causation of any distress contended to be exhibited by the child as to spending time with the father is a matter to be determined by the trial judge. In the shadow of the allocation of the trial dates in short compass, commencing on 25 May 2026, I am of the view that the order sought as to an urgent psychological assessment of the child would occasion a further examination of the child which would not be in the child’s best interests. The relief is otherwise devoid of necessary particulars. As currently constructed and prosecuted, it has no probity of value. That relief will be dismissed.
The father was unable to identify the person who would conduct the “joint father-child counselling sessions” sought to occur on a monthly basis between now and the trial to facilitate contact arrangements. That relief is forlorn for reasons not dissimilar to that identified in refusing the relief sought by paragraph 1 of the Amended Application in a Proceeding. That part of his relief will be refused.
The father encountered challenges in acknowledging that the order sought by him in paragraph 3 of his Amended Application in a Proceeding sought a variation of the orders made on 29 April 2025 as to the identity of the supervising contact service, as to the location of the supervised contact, and as to meeting the cost of the supervised contact. Irrespective of the merit of the father’s application, and as to this relief and his desire to resume supervised time spent with the child, there is no evidence that the proposed varied supervised contact location or service can be secured between now and the trial, and in those circumstances, there is little value in further considering the relief sought at this time. It will be refused.
The relief sought by the father in paragraph 4 of his Amended Application in a Proceeding is an attempt to confine the trial judge’s role and discretion in conducting the final parenting hearing, and specifically as to the factual determinations to be made by the primary judge. It directs an abuse of process and will be refused.
For all of the above reasons, the orders sought by way of paragraphs 1 through to 4 inclusive in the father’s Amended Application in a Proceeding filed on 22 December 2025 will be refused and the Amended Application in a Proceeding will be dismissed.
The prosecution of that part of the mother’s Response to an Application in a Proceeding seeking that the order for supervised time made on 29 April 2025 be discharged engages directly with the issues at trial. It is preferable that those issues be ventilated in a trial process where contested evidence can be the subject of cross-examination with the benefit of any supplementary oral evidence from the court child expert. In circumstances where the final trial is listed to commence approximately 13 weeks from today, it is in the interests of the child and the parties to determine the relief as efficiently as possible by consolidating it for determination at the final trial. Such order will be made.
NOTE:

These reasons have been corrected from the transcript. Topic headings have been inserted, and grammatical errors have been corrected. In addition, amendments have been made to make the orally delivered reasons clear and easy to read.
